summary: The author achieved 6th place by prioritizing high-quality pseudo-labeling over direct competition data training, aggregating approximately 560K samples from LMSYS, API-generated prompts, and open-source DPO datasets. They distilled knowledge from large teacher models (Llama3.3-70B, Qwen2.5-72B) into smaller student models (Gemma2-9B, Qwen2.5-14B) using LoRA and a combined KL divergence and cross-entropy loss. The final pipeline relied on a primary-supporting inference strategy optimized for a strict 12-hour time limit, while deliberately deprioritizing multilingual optimization to focus on English accuracy. Extensive experimentation revealed that techniques like TTA, LoRA merging, and CoT prompting failed to improve performance, highlighting the importance of data quality and careful inference routing.
extensive_summary:
### Pseudo-Labeling and Data Aggregation
The solution heavily relied on pseudo-labeling to bypass direct competition data training. The team aggregated approximately 560K samples by filtering short responses from three main sources: data generated by the 3rd-place LMSYS team, prompts sampled from a 1M dataset with API-generated responses, and around 10 open-source DPO datasets like RLHFlow. They experimented with two judging models for label generation: a baseline Gemma2-9B fine-tuned on competition data and an enhanced setup using Llama3.3-70B and Qwen2.5-72B. While the enhanced method offered minor gains, retraining Gemma2-9B with the pseudo-labeled data proved highly effective and achieved a strong LB score without direct competition data training.

### Knowledge Distillation and Training
Knowledge was distilled from teacher models (Qwen2.5-72B, Llama3.3-70B) into student models (Gemma2-9B, Qwen2.5-14B) using training data from WSDM and LMSYS. The distillation process employed an equally weighted average of KL Divergence Loss and Cross-Entropy Loss. Direct LoRA training was applied to 4-bit quantized models with a maximum sequence length of 2500 tokens, as extending to 3072 tokens offered no noticeable benefit relative to training time. Distillation notably improved Gemma2-9B but had minimal impact on Qwen2.5-14B.

### Inference Strategy and Multilingual Focus
The final deployment utilized a primary-supporting model architecture. Qwen2.5-14B served as the primary classifier using its logits, while Gemma2-9B was deployed selectively to handle cases where the primary model struggled. This routing mechanism was carefully managed to stay within a strict 12-hour inference time limit. Regarding language strategy, the team deprioritized broad multilingual performance since Gemma and Qwen are already strong multilingual models, instead focusing optimization on the top five languages, particularly English, which initially showed suboptimal accuracy.
competition_flow: Aggregated external prompts and DPO data to generate ~560K pseudo-labeled samples, distilled knowledge from large teacher models (Llama3.3-70B, Qwen2.5-72B) into smaller student models (Gemma2-9B, Qwen2.5-14B) via LoRA and KL/CE loss, and submitted predictions using a primary-supporting inference strategy optimized for a 12-hour time limit.
data_processing: Filtered short responses to obtain ~560K samples
Mixed 3rd-place LMSYS data, API-generated responses from a 1M prompt dataset, and ~10 open-source DPO datasets
Set maximum sequence length to 2500 tokens
Prioritized the top five languages with a specific focus on English
loss_functions: equally weighted average of KL Divergence Loss and Cross-Entropy Loss
ensembling: Used a primary-supporting model setup where Qwen2.5-14B provided primary logits and Gemma2-9B was deployed selectively for difficult cases; explicitly tested multi-LoRA ensembling but found it failed to improve performance.
insights: Pseudo-labeling with high-quality external data can yield strong leaderboard scores even without direct competition data training.
Multilingual performance should be deprioritized if base models are already strong multilingualizers, focusing instead on optimizing English accuracy.
Extending sequence length beyond 2500 tokens offers diminishing returns relative to training time constraints.
critical_findings: LoRA merging and post-training quantization caused significant, unresolved performance degradation.
Dynamic truncation during inference initially provided a small leaderboard boost but lost effectiveness after training code updates.
Original labels from DPO datasets were ineffective for direct post-training but highly valuable for pseudo-labeling.
what_did_not_work: TTA showed no measurable impact on sequence classification.
LoRA merge caused significant performance degradation.
Multi-LoRA ensemble approaches failed to improve performance.
Dynamic token allocation based on response length led to abnormal length distribution.
Chain-of-Thought prompting strategies did not yield meaningful improvements.
Dynamic truncation during inference initially provided a +0.003 LB boost but effectiveness diminished later.
Fine-tuning mDeBERTa for multiple-choice tasks resulted in poor performance due to parameter limitations.
Using GPT-3.5 as a judge model for pseudo-labeling yielded unsatisfactory results due to sensitivity and lack of transparency.
Few-shot prompting (2-shot to 32-shot) did not improve accuracy.
